title: Non-Maximally Entangled States for Quantum Key Distribution in Underwater Channels: BBM92 Protocol via Kraus Operators
authors: Nour Rizk, Angélique Drémeau, Arnaud Coatanhay
abstract: Underwater optical channels pose significant challenges to the security and reliability of quantum communication systems due to absorption and scattering. In this paper, we investigate the BBM92 entanglement-based quantum key distribution (QKD) protocol under realistic underwater channel conditions. Photon pairs are prepared in non-maximally entangled states, and the underwater propagation medium is modeled as a quantum channel incorporating both amplitude-damping and depolarizing effects, described within the Kraus operator formalism. The protocol performance is evaluated in terms of quantum bit error rate (QBER) and secret key rate (SKR), analyzed as functions of the entanglement degree and channel degradation parameters. Closed-form analytical expressions for the QBER and SKR are derived for the proposed channel model and validated through Monte Carlo simulations. The proposed framework is then applied to various realistic underwater scenarios, considering different water types, namely clear ocean, coastal, and turbid water, as well as varying atmospheric conditions.
summary: This paper analyzes how underwater environmental factors like turbidity and background light affect the security of the BBM92 quantum key distribution protocol. It specifically quantifies the performance degradation when using non-maximally entangled states. The results highlight the severe limitations on secure communication distances in turbid waters.
main_problem: Investigating the performance and security of the BBM92 entanglement-based QKD protocol in realistic underwater optical channels, specifically addressing the impact of non-maximally entangled states and channel degradation.
main_result: The study finds that increasing water turbidity and reducing the degree of entanglement both significantly decrease the secure transmission distance and Secret Key Rate (SKR).
method: The authors derive closed-form analytical expressions for QBER and SKR using the Kraus operator formalism and validate these results through Monte Carlo simulations.
model_or_system: An entanglement-based BBM92 QKD system where a source generates non-maximally entangled polarization states, propagating through an underwater channel modeled with amplitude-damping and depolarizing Kraus operators.
key_observables: Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER) and Secret Key Rate (SKR).
important_parameters: Entanglement parameter (beta), water turbidity (clear, coastal, turbid), atmospheric/background noise levels, and transmission distance (L).
main_assumptions: The source is a well-collimated laser beam with negligible divergence, and the primary channel effects considered are amplitude damping and depolarization rather than all forms of turbulence.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the protocol schematic; Fig 2 and 3 illustrate QBER vs. distance for different entanglement degrees and water types; Fig 4(b) shows the impact of reduced entanglement on the SKR.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of underwater QKD, defines the physical model and Kraus-based channel, derives analytical expressions for error rates, validates them via Monte Carlo simulation, and evaluates performance across various environmental scenarios.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to researchers in quantum optics and open quantum systems as it applies the Kraus operator formalism to model decoherence and photon loss in a complex, realistic environment.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Schematic representation of the BBM92 QKD protocol in an underwater channel, in which the orientation θi of each HWP sets the angle of the associated measurement basis.
Fig. 3: QBERBBM92-non-max vs. distance for different scenarios, with β = π/5 in (a) clear water, (b) coastal water, and (c) turbid water.
Fig. 2: QBERBBM92-non-max vs. distance for different scenarios, with β = π/4, corresponding to the maximally entangled case, in (a) clear water, (b) coastal water, and (c) turbid water.

Fig. 3 presents the QBER of the BBM92 protocol as a func- tion of the transmission distance for a non-maximally entangled state (β = π/5) in a non-turbulent underwater channel, considering three water types under ﬁve atmospheric scenarios. In all cases, the QBER increases with distance, while the achievable secure range is substantially reduced compared with the maximally entangled case shown in Fig. 2. This behavior reﬂects the lower robustness of non- maximally entangled states against propagation losses and noise, which makes the quantum correlations more vulnerable to environ- mental degradation. Fig. 2 shows the QBER of the BBM92 protocol as functions of distance L for a maximally entangled state, for three types of water under ﬁve atmospheric scenarios. The correspondence between Monte Carlo simulations and analytical results validates proposed model. In all cases, the QBER increases with distance and ultimately becomes dominated by background-photon noise. As shown in Fig. 2(a), clear ocean water provides the longest secure transmission range among the three media. In this case, the QBER remains below the 11% security threshold up to approxi- mately 3.05 m in Scenario 1, whereas in Scenario 5 the threshold is reached at about 2.73 m.
